subject: Physics
q:
The volume charge density of a sphere of radius 6 m is 2 μC cm-3. The number of lines of force per unit surface area coming out from the surface of the sphere is _____ ×1010 N C-1.

[Given : Permittivity of vacuum ϵ0=8.85×10-12C2 N-1-m-2]
question:
The volume charge density of a sphere of radius 6 m is 2 μC cm-3. The number of lines of force per unit surface area coming out from the surface of the sphere is _____ ×1010 N C-1.

[Given : Permittivity of vacuum ϵ0=8.85×10-12C2 N-1-m-2]
solution:
Given here, ρ=2 μC cm-3 and R=6 m.

Number of lines of force per unit area = electric field at surface=KQR2

E=14πε0ρ43πR3R2 

E=ρR3ϵ0=2×63×8.85×10-12=0.45×1012 N C-1

=45×1010 N C-1

subject: Physics
q: A long cylindrical volume contains a uniformly distributed charge of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math>. The radius of cylindrical volume is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. A charge particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>q</mi></mfenced></math> revolves around the cylinder in a circular path. The kinetic energy of the particle is :
question: A long cylindrical volume contains a uniformly distributed charge of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math>. The radius of cylindrical volume is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. A charge particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>q</mi></mfenced></math> revolves around the cylinder in a circular path. The kinetic energy of the particle is :
solution:
Electric field due to a long cylinder is,

E=λ2πε0r=ρA2πε0r

If a charge q is rotating in a circle of radius r

mv2r=qE  ⇒mv2r=qρA2πε0r⇒12mv2=qρπR24πε0=qρR24ε0

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following correctly represents the variation of electric potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>)</mo></math> of a charged spherical conductor of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>)</mo></math> with radial distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> from the centre?
question: Which of the following correctly represents the variation of electric potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>)</mo></math> of a charged spherical conductor of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>)</mo></math> with radial distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> from the centre?
solution:
As electric field inside the charged conducting sphere is zero, therefore potential will be constant for the inside part.

For outside part, electric potential decreases as V=kqr and the conducting sphere behaves like a point charge concentrated at the centre.

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> each are placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> apart. A third point charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from mid-point on the perpendicular bisector. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> at which charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> will experience the maximum Coulomb's force is:
question: Two point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> each are placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> apart. A third point charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> from mid-point on the perpendicular bisector. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> at which charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> will experience the maximum Coulomb's force is:
solution:
The distance between q and Q is r=x2+d22=x2+d24.

The force between q and Q will be F=kqQr2.

From the given figure we can see that only vertical components of the forces will add, and the horizontal components will get cancelled out. Therefore,

Fnet=2kqQr2cosθ=2kqQr2×xr=kqQxx2+d2432

For force to be maximum, dFnetdx=0.

⇒kqQx2+d2432-x×32x2+d2412×2xx2+d24322=0

⇒x2+d24=3x2⇒2x2=d24⇒x=d22

subject: Physics
q: Shown in the figure is a shell made of a conductor. It has inner radius a and outer radius b, and carries charge Q . At its centre a dipole p→ is placed as shown then:
question: Shown in the figure is a shell made of a conductor. It has inner radius a and outer radius b, and carries charge Q . At its centre a dipole p→ is placed as shown then:
options: Surface charge density on the inner surface of the shell is zero everywhere.
Electric field outside the shell is the same as that of a point charge at the centre of the shell.

Surface charge density on the inner surface is uniform and equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mo>(</mo><mstyle displaystyle="true"><mfrac><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mstyle><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></math>.

Surface charge density on the outer surface depends on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|" separators="|"><mover accent="true"><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math>
solution: Net charge on dipole is zero, so it will not affect the charge distribution and electric field on the outside but the charge distribution inside the shell will disturbed.

subject: Physics
q: A two point charges 4q and –q are fixed on the x -axis at x=−d2 and x=d2, respectively. If the third point charge ‘q ’ is taken from the origin to x=d along the semicircle as shown in the figure, the energy of the charge will:
question: A two point charges 4q and –q are fixed on the x -axis at x=−d2 and x=d2, respectively. If the third point charge ‘q ’ is taken from the origin to x=d along the semicircle as shown in the figure, the energy of the charge will:
solution:
Potential at origin O, the potential due to two charges is given by

V0=4q4πε0d2+−q4πε0d2=6q4πε0d

Potential at P at a distance, x=d is given by

VP=4q4πε03d2+−q4πε0d2=q6πε0d

Change in potential energy due the charge motion of charge q is given by

 ∆U=qΔV=qVf−Vi

⇒∆U=qVP−VO

∆U=qq6πε0d−3q2πε0d=−4q23πε0d

subject: Physics
q: For a uniformly charged thin spherical shell, the electric potential V radially away from the centre O of shell can be graphically represented as
question: For a uniformly charged thin spherical shell, the electric potential V radially away from the centre O of shell can be graphically represented as
solution:
The potential is constant inside the sphere as the electric field is zero inside the sphere. So, the value is given by 

V=Q4πε0R for r<R

The potential at the surface is 

V=Q4πε0R

The potential outside the sphere starts decreasing and is given by the formula 

V=Q4πε0r for r>R

subject: Physics
q: C1 and C2 are two hollow concentric cubes enclosing charges 2Q and 3Q respectively as shown in figure. The ratio of electric flux passing through C1 and C2 is:
question: C1 and C2 are two hollow concentric cubes enclosing charges 2Q and 3Q respectively as shown in figure. The ratio of electric flux passing through C1 and C2 is:
solution:
Gauss's law states that the net flux of an electric field in a closed surface is directly proportional to the enclosed electric charge. Therefore,

ϕsmaller cube =2Qε0

Since the smaller cube is inside the bigger cube, so total charge contained by the bigger cube will be 2Q+3Q=5Q.

ϕbigger cube =5Qε0

Therefore, required ratio will be, 

ϕsmaller cube ϕbigger cube =25.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason(R)
Assertion (A) : Work done by electric field on moving a positive charge on an equipotential surface is always zero.
Reason (R) : Electric lines of forces are always perpendicular to equipotential surfaces.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason(R)
Assertion (A) : Work done by electric field on moving a positive charge on an equipotential surface is always zero.
Reason (R) : Electric lines of forces are always perpendicular to equipotential surfaces.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct
Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
The equipotential surfaces are surfaces where the electric potential is the same at every point. In other words, there is no potential difference along an equipotential surface. Since work done (W) is given by the equation W=q∆V, and there is no potential difference on an equipotential surface, the work done is always zero.

As there is no force along the surface of the equipotential surface, the electric field must be perpendicular to the surface.

Hence, while both statements are correct, the reason (R) is providing the correct explanation for why the work done on a positive charge on an equipotential surface is always zero.

question: Figure shows a rod AB, which is bent in a 120° circular arc of radius R. A charge (-Q) is uniformly distributed over rod AB. What is the electric filed E→ at the centre of curvature O ?
solution:
δ=2kλRsinθ2i^

=2kRQ2Rπ3sin60°i^

=3kQπR232i^

=332πkQR2i^

subject: Physics
q: A positive charge particle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mg</mi></math> is thrown in opposite direction to a uniform electric field of strength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the charge on the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and the initial velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, how much distance it will travel before coming to the rest momentarily
question: A positive charge particle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mg</mi></math> is thrown in opposite direction to a uniform electric field of strength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the charge on the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and the initial velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, how much distance it will travel before coming to the rest momentarily
solution:
Force experienced by a particle with charge q in an electric field F=qE.

Now, the acceleration produced is given as a=Fm=qEm=40×10-6×1051×10-4

=4×104 m  s-2  (As the particle is projected against the electric field, hence it is decelerated)

Using, v2=u2-2as

We have, 02=u2-2as

s=v22a=20022×4×104=0.5 m

subject: Physics
q:
This question has statement $1$ and statement $2$ . Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
An insulating solid sphere of radius $\mathrm{R}$ has a uniformly positive charge density $\rho$. As a result of this uniform charge distribution there is a finite value of electric potential at the centre of the sphere, at the surface of the sphere and also at a point out side the sphere. The electric potential at infinity is zero.
Statement $1$: When a charge $q$ is taken from the centre to the surface of the sphere, its potential energy changes by $\frac{\mathrm{qp}}{3 \varepsilon_0}$
Statement $2$: The electric field at a distance $r(r < R)$ from the centre of the sphere is $\frac{\rho r}{3 \varepsilon_0}$
question:
This question has statement $1$ and statement $2$ . Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
An insulating solid sphere of radius $\mathrm{R}$ has a uniformly positive charge density $\rho$. As a result of this uniform charge distribution there is a finite value of electric potential at the centre of the sphere, at the surface of the sphere and also at a point out side the sphere. The electric potential at infinity is zero.
Statement $1$: When a charge $q$ is taken from the centre to the surface of the sphere, its potential energy changes by $\frac{\mathrm{qp}}{3 \varepsilon_0}$
Statement $2$: The electric field at a distance $r(r < R)$ from the centre of the sphere is $\frac{\rho r}{3 \varepsilon_0}$
options: Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is true, Statement $2$ is not the correct explanation for statement $1$.
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is false
Statement $1$ is false, Statement $2$ is true
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is the correct explanation for statement $1$
solution:
$\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}} \mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}\left(\rho \times \frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3\right)$
$\mathrm{E}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{r}}{3 \varepsilon_0}$
Statement $2$ is correct
$\Delta \mathrm{PE}=\left(\mathrm{V}_{\text {sur }}-\mathrm{V}_{\text {cent }}\right) \mathrm{q}=-\frac{\mathrm{q}}{6 \varepsilon_0} \rho \mathrm{R}^2$
Statement $1$ is incorrect

subject: Physics
q:
Choose the incorrect statement:
(a) The electric lines of force entering into a Gaussian surface provide negative flux.
(b) A charge q is placed at the centre of a cube. The flux through all the faces will be the same.
(c) In a uniform electric field net flux through a closed Gaussian surface containing no net charge, is zero.
(d) When an electric field is parallel to a Gaussian surface, it provides a finite non-zero flux.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Choose the incorrect statement:
(a) The electric lines of force entering into a Gaussian surface provide negative flux.
(b) A charge q is placed at the centre of a cube. The flux through all the faces will be the same.
(c) In a uniform electric field net flux through a closed Gaussian surface containing no net charge, is zero.
(d) When an electric field is parallel to a Gaussian surface, it provides a finite non-zero flux.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
We know that electric flux is a scalar quantity and this is the scalar product of the magnitude of electric field and surface area. 

Recall, the formula given by Gauss theorem, ϕ=E→·A→=EAcosθ, here θ=90°, it means, ϕ=0.

subject: Physics
q: The electrostatic potential due to an electric dipole at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> varies as :
question: The electrostatic potential due to an electric dipole at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> varies as :
solution:
The potential at a distance r due to a short dipole is Vdipr=14πε∘ p→·r^r2, where p→=dipole moment.

Thus, Vdip(r) is proportional to 1r2.

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn></math> identical drops are charged at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> each. They combine to form a bigger drop. The potential of the bigger drop will be _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn></math> identical drops are charged at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> each. They combine to form a bigger drop. The potential of the bigger drop will be _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
solution:
Total volume of the drop will remain unchanged,

So,  27×43πr3=43πR3

⇒R=3r

Potential of the small drops, 22=kqr

For bigger drop, potential will be

V=k27q3r=9kqr=9×22=198 V

q: A point charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is placed at the origin. At what location on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis should a point charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> be placed so that the net electric field is zero at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis ?
question: A point charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is placed at the origin. At what location on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis should a point charge of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> be placed so that the net electric field is zero at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis ?
solution:
Let the charge 40 μC be placed at a distance X0 from the origin as shown in the figure.

For the electric field at x=2 to be 0,

EP=0⇒k×1022=k×40 X0-22

⇒12=2X0-2

⇒X0-2=4

Therefore, the charge is located at X0=4+2=6 cm

2=kqr

q:

Statement 1: No work is required to be done to move a test charge between any two points on an equipotential surface.
Statement 2 : Electric lines of force at the equipotential surfaces are mutually perpendicular to each other.
question:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.

Statement 1: No work is required to be done to move a test charge between any two points on an equipotential surface.
Statement 2 : Electric lines of force at the equipotential surfaces are mutually perpendicular to each other.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
solution:
The work done is moving a charge along an equipotential surface is always zero.
The direction of electric field is perpendicular to the equipotential surface or lines.

q: Two identical charged spheres are suspended by strings of equal lengths. The string make an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with each other. When suspended in a liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle remains same. If density of material of the sphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the dielectric constant of the liquid is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: Two identical charged spheres are suspended by strings of equal lengths. The string make an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with each other. When suspended in a liquid of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle remains same. If density of material of the sphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the dielectric constant of the liquid is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><mn>37</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>3</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
For first case:

Tcosθ=mg and 

Tsinθ=Fe

Therefore, tanθ=Femg

tanθ=FeρBVg....(i)

For second case:

tanθ=FekρBVg-ρLVg.....(ii) 

From Eq. (i) & (ii), we get

ρBVg=ρB-ρLkVg

⇒1.4 = 0.7 k 

⇒k = 2

subject: Physics
q: Two isolated metallic solid spheres of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> are charged such that both have same charge density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math> . The spheres are then connected by a thin conducting wire. If the new charge density of the bigger sphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>σ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>σ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mi>σ</mi></mfrac></math> is :
question: Two isolated metallic solid spheres of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math> are charged such that both have same charge density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi></math> . The spheres are then connected by a thin conducting wire. If the new charge density of the bigger sphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>σ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>σ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mi>σ</mi></mfrac></math> is :
solution:
Charge and charge density on both isolated metallic solid spheres are show below.

Let the charges and the charge densities on both the spheres after connected by thin conducting wire be Q1', Q2', σ and σ' as shown below.

Once connected, charge on both the spheres will redistribute such that potentials at the surface of both spheres is the same.

∴kQ1'R=kQ2'2R

⇒Q1'4πε0R=Q2'4πε02R

∴Q2'=2Q1'

Using charge conservation, 

Q1'+Q2'=Q1+Q2

∴Q2'2+Q2'=20πR2σ

32Q2'=20πR2σ

∴Q2'4π2R2=23×20πR2σ16πR2

∴σ'σ=56

subject: Physics
q: Two particles A and B having charges 20 μC and -5 μC respectively are held fixed with a separation of 5 cm At what position a third charged particle should be placed so that it does not experience a net electric force ?
question: Two particles A and B having charges 20 μC and -5 μC respectively are held fixed with a separation of 5 cm At what position a third charged particle should be placed so that it does not experience a net electric force ?
options: At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> on the right side

At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> on the left side of system

At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> between two charges
solution:
Charge should be placed near -5μC at x distance

K(20)Q(5+x)2=K(5)QX2
4x2=(5+x)2
4x2=25+x2+10x
3x2-10x-25=0 ⇒x=5

subject: Physics
q: Four point charges <math><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> and <math><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> are placed on y-axis at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. The magnitude of the electric field <math><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></math> at a point on the x-axis at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mo>,</mo></math> with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mo>≫</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> will behave as:
question: Four point charges <math><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> and <math><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> are placed on y-axis at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. The magnitude of the electric field <math><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></math> at a point on the x-axis at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mo>,</mo></math> with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mo>≫</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> will behave as:
solution:
The electric field at P is superposition of the fields due to all four charges. The electric field due to the two +q charges is

E+q=KqD2+d2cos⁡θ1×2
E-q=KqD2+4d2cos⁡θ2×2
The field due to +q is towards right and the field due to -q is towards left. Also θ1≈0 and θ2≈0 .
∴Enet=E+q-E-q≈KqD2+d2-KqD2+4d2
=Kq 3d2D2+d2D2+4d2≈3Kqd2D4
E∝1D4

subject: Physics
q:
A uniformly charged solid sphere of radius R has potential V0 (measured with respect to ∞ ) on its surface. For this sphere the equipotential surfaces with potential 3V02,5V04,3V04 and V04 have radius R1 , R2, R3 and R4 respectively. Then

Note : This question had two option correct at the time of examination. Proper corrections are made in the question to avoid it.
question:
A uniformly charged solid sphere of radius R has potential V0 (measured with respect to ∞ ) on its surface. For this sphere the equipotential surfaces with potential 3V02,5V04,3V04 and V04 have radius R1 , R2, R3 and R4 respectively. Then

Note : This question had two option correct at the time of examination. Proper corrections are made in the question to avoid it.
solution:
Potential for uniformly charged solid sphere

v = 1 4 π ε 0 Q r       outside i.e r > R

v = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R   on the surface

v = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 r 2 R 2   inside i.e. r < R

Clearly

   3 v 0 2 , 5 v 0 4  are inside potentials ∵ > v 0

3 v 0 4 , v 0 4  are outside potentials ∵ < v 0

To get R 1 : 3 v 0 2 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 1 2 R 2

where, v 0 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R

3 2 × 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 1 2 R 2

3 2 = 3 2 - 1 2 R 1 2 R 2 ⇒ R 1 = 0

To get R 2 : 5 4 v 0 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 2 2 R 2

5 4 1 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 2 2 R 2

5 4 = 3 2 - 1 2 R 2 2 R 2

1 2 R 2 2 R 2 = 1 4  ⇒ R 2 = R 2

To get R 3 : 3 v 0 4 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3

3 4 1 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3

3 4 R = 1 R 3

R 3 = 4 3 R

To get R 4 : v 0 4 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 4

1 4 × 1 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 4

R 4 = 4 R

R 4 - R 3 = 4 R - 4 R 3 = 8 R 3 > R 2

R 1 = 0  and R 2 < R 4 - R 3

subject: Physics
q: The electric field due to a short electric dipole at a large distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>r</mi></mfenced></math> from center of dipole on the equatorial plane varies with distance as :
question: The electric field due to a short electric dipole at a large distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>r</mi></mfenced></math> from center of dipole on the equatorial plane varies with distance as :
solution:
An electric dipole is a system of two charges of equal and opposite magnitude placed at a short distance.

The dipole moment is, p=2ql.

The electric field due to the dipole is,

E=2kpr3

Clearly, the electric field varies as 1r3.

The bob of a simple pendulum has mass 2 g and a charge of 5.0 μC. It is at rest in a uniform horizontal electric field of intensity 2000 V/m At equilibrium, the angle that the pendulum makes with the vertical is:
take g=10 m/s2
question:
The bob of a simple pendulum has mass 2 g and a charge of 5.0 μC. It is at rest in a uniform horizontal electric field of intensity 2000 V/m At equilibrium, the angle that the pendulum makes with the vertical is:
take g=10 m/s2
solution:
tan⁡θ=QEmg=5×10-6×20002×10-3×10=12
⇒θ=tan-1⁡0.5

Alternate charges<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>9</mn></math> have charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> each, while <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>10</mn></math> have charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>–</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> each. The potential V and the electric field E at the centre of the circle are respectively : (Take<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at infinity)
solution:
vnet=5kqR+5k(-q)R
vnet=0, Qnet=0
Enet=0, by symmetry

question: Determine the electric dipole moment of the system of three charges, placed on the vertices of an equilateral triangle, as shown in the figure:

subject: Physics
q: An electron is released from rest near an infinite non-conducting sheet of uniform charge density ' $-\sigma$ '. The rate of change of de-Broglie wave length associated with the electron varies inversely as $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ power of time. The numerical value of $n$ is ________.
question: An electron is released from rest near an infinite non-conducting sheet of uniform charge density ' $-\sigma$ '. The rate of change of de-Broglie wave length associated with the electron varies inversely as $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ power of time. The numerical value of $n$ is ________.
solution:
Let the momentum of $\mathrm{e}^{-}$at any time t is p and its de-broglie wavelength is $\lambda$.
Then, $\mathrm{p}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{dp}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{-\mathrm{h}}{\lambda^2} \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \mathrm{ma}=\mathrm{F}=-\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda} \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}} \quad[\mathrm{~m}=\text { mass of } \mathrm{e}]
\end{aligned}$
Where, -ve sign represents decrease in $\lambda$ with time
$\mathrm{ma}=\frac{-\mathrm{h}}{(\mathrm{~h} / \mathrm{p})^2} \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{a}=-\frac{\mathrm{p}^2}{\mathrm{mh}} \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \mathrm{a}=-\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{~h}} \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{\mathrm{ah}}{\mathrm{mv}^2}...(1)
\end{aligned}$
here, $\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{qE}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}} \frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}$
$\mathrm{a}=\frac{\sigma \mathrm{e}}{2 \mathrm{~m} \varepsilon_0}$
and $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{u}+\mathrm{at}$
$\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{at}$
Substituting values of a \& v in equation (1)
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{2 \mathrm{~h} \varepsilon_0}{\sigma \mathrm{et}^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} \lambda}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=2
\end{aligned}$
